nativeRange.habitat: Shallow Caribbean reefs and back-reef areas with moderate to strong water movement and moderate light; commonly found on reef slopes, rubble zones, and sandy lagoon floors

decorPreferences: adequate spacing from neighboring corals and anemones
open water column positioning for flow exposure

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been found to benefit from supplemental feedings several times per week with fine particle filter-feeding foods such as live or frozen baby brine shrimp, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan, and other suitable micro-plankton or phytoplankton preparations. Supplementation with iodine, calcium, strontium, and other trace elements is generally recommended.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals placed in direct contact
heavy algae producers (algae can colonize skeleton rapidly)

summary: Eunicea is a genus of ~25–34 Caribbean photosynthetic gorgonian sea rods prized for their branching, knobby colonies. Intermediate care; needs high, non-laminar flow and moderate lighting to prevent algae colonization.
careNotes:
Note: Eunicea is a genus of approximately 25–34 described species (sources vary between ~25 and ~35) traded collectively under names such as 'Knobby Sea Rod,' 'Purple Tree Gorgonian,' and 'Sea Rod.' The scientificName field is set to 'Eunicea sp.' (genus level, no species epithet) to reflect this trade convention, consistent with how the genus is sold in the aquarium hobby.

Eunicea gorgonians are photosynthetic Caribbean soft corals belonging to family Plexauridae. They are among the more keeper-accessible gorgonians because their zooxanthellae partially sustain them, unlike non-photosynthetic gorgonians that require near-constant heavy feeding.

Key care requirements:
• Flow: Strong, turbulent (non-laminar) flow is essential and tends to be the single most critical factor for long-term survival. Gorgonians are highly susceptible to algae and cyanobacteria colonizing the exposed skeleton; strong, randomized flow is the primary defense. LiveAquaria advises 'medium to strong, constant or intermittent' flow. The controller brief specifies HIGH flow; this is broadly consistent with best practices for the genus. Flow deflected from multiple angles (gyre-type or powerhead cross-flow) is generally preferred over direct laminar flow.
• Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally advised (approximately 100–200 PAR). These are back-reef / shallow-Caribbean corals that have been observed thriving under moderate reef lighting. Placement in the middle to upper portions of the aquarium is commonly recommended to achieve appropriate light exposure.
• Feeding: Although photosynthetic, supplemental feeding several times per week with micro-plankton (phytoplankton, frozen baby brine shrimp, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan) has been reported to improve growth and colony health.
• Algae management: The most common cause of failure in captivity is algae or cyanobacteria colonizing and smothering the coral skeleton. Affected areas should be gently cleaned with a soft brush; brief freshwater dips can be used for more severe cases. Maintaining strong flow and stable water quality reduces this risk significantly.
• Anchoring: The base of the colony should be anchored to live rock with underwater epoxy, as gorgonians do not encrust and can tip or be swept by strong flow.
• Colony size: Wild Eunicea colonies typically reach 30–60 cm in height; aquarium specimens sold at 8–12 inches (20–30 cm) are common. Rapid growth has been reported under good conditions.
• Supplementation: Regular additions of iodine, calcium, strontium, and trace elements are generally recommended.
• Reef safe: Generally considered reef safe; no sweeper tentacles or aggressive chemical allelopathy documented, though adequate spacing from all neighbors is advised.
• The genus encompasses roughly 25–34 valid species per different authorities (LiveAquaria listing, Wikipedia/WoRMS). All share near-identical husbandry requirements.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural Caribbean reefs. Captive sexual breeding is not commonly reported in the hobby. Fragmentation (cutting branches and reattaching with epoxy) is the primary captive propagation method and has been reported to succeed when fragments are large enough and water quality is maintained.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Purple Tree Gorgonian (Eunicea sp.)
primary.notes: Species page confirmed scientific name Eunicea sp., family Plexauridae. Provided temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, moderate lighting, medium-to-strong flow, supplemental feeding several times/week, care level moderate, Caribbean native range.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Sea Fans collection (Purple Candelabra Gorgonian cross-check)
additional.notes: Cross-checked high-stakes parameters: temperature 72–78°F (consistent), SG 1.023–1.025 (consistent), pH 8.1–8.4 (consistent), dKH 8–12 (consistent), moderate lighting (consistent), medium-to-strong flow (consistent). Confirms supplemental feeding and iodine/calcium/strontium supplementation requirements.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Plexauridae, Order: Malacalcyonacea, Class: Octocorallia), species count (~34 per WoRMS), and habitat context (back-reef areas, brightly lit zones). Not used for husbandry parameters.
